better than, uh, they are for building, um, like mobile apps. So here we've got, like, Claude Desktop,
or it could be Cursor, or it could be Codex. They're very similar apps.

And, like, one thing that just makes this incredible is that it has this, uh, in app browser right here. So if I were, you know, to I don't I don't even know if I have WiFi right now, but don't know, send some message or something, reply, whatever. And
then, the agent has just built into it. When you're inside of the desktop app, it has

iOS and building a mobile app has none of this.
The equivalent, when you're building a mobile app, would be to fire off a bunch of exec commands to, like, sim control if you already have all that installed,

And and then it's slow and it has all these weird pieces, like when you take a screenshot of the browser, it, you know, resizes it down so it uses fewer tokens. It's the opposite when you take a screenshot of an iOS simulator, it's just full res.
Um, so it's pretty painful.

naturally,

you could just build that same MCP and then add a bunch of skills and a bunch of system prompt and run a bunch of evals for every single tool. But I had the idea. What if we just put the iOS simulator inside of the browser?

on top of just being able to now develop inside of here, you know, Claude can use its MCP

know, like, why do we build iOS apps? Right? Like, or just native apps in general instead of mobile websites? It's usually for the hardware features.

the iOS simulator does not have certain hardware features. Like, it doesn't have a camera built into it. I want to talk about mobile verification with agents and really just how I'm building iOS apps with Cloud Desktop and the Codex app right now.
So I'm Evan Bacon.
I am the head of AI at Expo.
And let's see what we got here.
I've just got a couple slides.
So, uh, agents are really, really great at building websites, like disproportionately better than, uh, they are for building, um, like mobile apps.
So here we've got, like, Claude Desktop, or it could be Cursor, or it could be Codex.
They're very similar apps.
And, like, one thing that just makes this incredible is that it has this, uh, in app browser right here.
So if I were, you know, to I don't I don't even know if I have WiFi right now, but don't know, send some message or something, reply, whatever.
And then, the agent has just built into it.
When you're inside of the desktop app, it has an MCP.
So, it can interact with the page, read the page, reload.
You can even do little things like you can, I don't know, like annotate and draw little pictures?
I don't I don't really know, like yeah.
You you can you you get the idea.
And then you can, like, add it to chat.
Uh, so there's just like iOS and building a mobile app has none of this.

The equivalent, when you're building a mobile app, would be to fire off a bunch of exec commands to, like, sim control if you already have all that installed, And and then it's slow and it has all these weird pieces, like when you take a screenshot of the browser, it, you know, resizes it down so it uses fewer tokens.
It's the opposite when you take a screenshot of an iOS simulator, it's just full res.
Um, so it's pretty painful.
Now, naturally, you could just build that same MCP and then add a bunch of skills and a bunch of system prompt and run a bunch of evals for every single tool.
But I had the idea.
What if we just put the iOS simulator inside of the browser?
So I made a command, npx serve-sim.
That's it.
That's all you have to run.
And then you're able to just open it inside of the browser.
So here, we have our iOS simulator running inside of Claude Desktop.
And this is running full, you know, all the frames in the world.
I guess it's a little choppy on the this whatever.
Ignore that.
You can go in here.
You can, like, you know, interact with it.
You've got all the multi touch, everything you want.
Uh, and then the agent can, uh, interact with this itself.
You can even draw all over the screen.
Right?
Which is, yeah, groundbreaking stuff.
So let's go over to the, uh, let's see.
Let's swing over to this chat app that I've been developing here on the side.
You know, same app.
This is actually built with Expo.
This tool works with anything, not just Expo or React Native.
Yeah.
See, no WiFi, whatever.
Live demos are so good, man.
That wasn't really part of it's not relevant to the demo.
Uh, but we also have some other tools over here, which are pretty fantastic.

It can send touches the same way it would with the browser.
We send all of the logs from the simulator to the console logs so it can query logs.
This one's actually pretty cool.
There's an inspect element feature on the page.
This was contributed by a member of the OpenAI team so that it could, you know, like, interact with the page and, like, find elements to click on and select.

Now, okay, one thing that's pretty sweet is, know, like, why do we build iOS apps?
Right?

It's usually for the hardware features.

Like, an agent can't simply run-in a loop and develop a camera app from scratch, which is a little wild.
So what I did was I added the ability here to well, here, let me show you what it looks like when you open the camera in the simulator.
You get just like a black screen.
So I added the ability here to, uh, run play on our device, and it will inject a binary, relaunch the app.
Let's see if we can actually get in here.
This might be a little tricky with the WiFi.
Let's see.
Okay.
Yeah.
So, we are inside of here.
We can open our camera and now we have this default generative video to emulate what it would be like if there was a camera feed, but we can also, um, just attach our actual, like, webcam.
Let's see if this works.

Well, let's see if it works.
